patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v3 4/4] btrfs: cap shrink_delalloc iterations to 128M

version_history.subject: [PATCH v3 4/4] btrfs: cap shrink_delalloc iterations to 128M

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch limits the iterations of the `shrink_delalloc` function in Btrfs to prevent it from blocking indefinitely, especially when dealing with large amounts of writeback data. The function is modified to process delalloc work in units of 128MiB, rather than trying to reclaim large chunks at once. This change aims to strike a balance between making progress on space reclaim and avoiding arbitrarily long syscall latencies and task hangs.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v3 3/4] btrfs: make inode->outstanding_extents a u64

version_history.subject: [PATCH v3 3/4] btrfs: make inode->outstanding_extents a u64

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch updates the data type of 'outstanding_extents' in the Btrfs inode structure from u32 to u64. This change is motivated by the fact that the maximum file size is LLONG_MAX, which results in a computation bound above by ~2^63 / 2^27. With recent changes to divide by 128KiB in compressed cases, this bound is even higher. The patch also updates the 'mod_outstanding_extents' function to handle u64 inputs and adds assertions to ensure that the inputs are valid. This change aims to capture the intent of the types and make the code more robust.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v3 2/4] btrfs: account for compression in delalloc extent reservation

version_history.subject: [PATCH v3 2/4] btrfs: account for compression in delalloc extent reservation

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch addresses an issue in the Btrfs file system where delayed reference reservations were under-estimated due to compression. Specifically, when COMPRESS_FORCE is set or the inode is configured to always compress, the maximum compressed extent size (128KiB) is significantly smaller than the maximum uncompressed extent size (128MiB). The patch introduces a new function `btrfs_inode_max_extents` that takes into account the compression type and returns the correct number of extents for reservation. This change prevents under-reservation of delayed refs reservations for delalloc writes, even when compression is forced. Additionally, the patch removes an unnecessary shim function `count_max_extents` and simplifies the code in the process.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v3 1/4] btrfs: reserve space for delayed_refs in delalloc

version_history.subject: [PATCH v3 1/4] btrfs: reserve space for delayed_refs in delalloc

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch addresses an issue in the Btrfs file system where space is not reserved for delayed references (delayed_refs) during delalloc operations. Delalloc uses a per-inode block reservation to anticipate metadata needs, but it does not account for delayed refs. As a result, when writeback occurs and delayed refs are generated, they draw from an under-reserved trans_handle's delayed refs reservation. This can lead to incorrect assessments of reserved space in the metadata space reclaim logic, causing poor decision making and blocking file system operations.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v3 0/4] btrfs: improve stalls under sudden writeback

version_history.subject: [PATCH v3 0/4] btrfs: improve stalls under sudden writeback

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series aims to improve stalls under sudden writeback in Btrfs file systems by addressing issues related to delalloc work and metadata space system. The patches introduce targeted improvements, including better reservations for delayed refs and smoothing of the work in flush_space(). This is achieved through four patches: (1) reserving space for delayed refs in delalloc, (2) accounting for compression in delalloc extent reservation, (3) making inode->outstanding_extents a u64, and (4) capping shrink_delalloc iterations. The changes are designed to reduce stalls on systems with large memory and disk sizes, where sudden writeback can cause system-wide issues.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v3] btrfs: btrfs_log_dev_io_error() on all bio errors

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2] btrfs: btrfs_log_dev_io_error() on all bio errors
[PATCH v3] btrfs: btrfs_log_dev_io_error() on all bio errors

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch modifies the btrfs_log_dev_io_error() function to log device I/O errors for all bio error codes, not just BLK_STS_IOERR and BLK_STS_TARGET. The change is motivated by the observation that some devices return unexpected error codes, leading to incorrect conclusions about system behavior.

review_comments.author: Christoph Hellwig
review_comments.summary: The patch looks good and is ready for merge. However, Christoph suggests using a switch statement in the second pass for clarity.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Mon, Apr 06, 2026 at 09:15:15AM -0700, Boris Burkov wrote:
> As far as I can tell, we never intentionally constrained ourselves to
> these status codes, and it is misleading and surprising to lack the
> bdev error logging when we get a different error code from the block
> layer. This can lead to jumping to a wrong conclusion like "this
> system didn't see any bio failures but aborted with EIO".
> 
> For example on nvme devices, I observe many failures coming back as
> BLK_STS_MEDIUM. It is apparent that the nvme driver returns a variety of
> BLK_STS_* status values in nvme_error_status().
> 
> So handle the known expected errors and make some noise on the rest
> which we expect won't really happen.

Looks good:

Reviewed-by: Christoph Hellwig <hch@lst.de>

although now that you have an unlikely annotation for the non-ok
slow patch I'd use a switch statement for clarity int the second
pass.

review_comments.author: Anand Jain
review_comments.summary: Anand points out that BLK_STS_PROTECTION is interpreted differently depending on the device type and suggests that the patch is NVMe-specific.
review_comments.raw_body:
On 7/4/26 00:15, Boris Burkov wrote:
> As far as I can tell, we never intentionally constrained ourselves to
> these status codes, and it is misleading and surprising to lack the
> bdev error logging when we get a different error code from the block
> layer. This can lead to jumping to a wrong conclusion like "this
> system didn't see any bio failures but aborted with EIO".
> 

> For example on nvme devices, I observe many failures coming back as
> BLK_STS_MEDIUM. It is apparent that the nvme driver returns a variety of
> BLK_STS_* status values in nvme_error_status().


BLK_STS_PROTECTION is interpreted differently depending on the device.
In NVMe, it indicates invalid Protection Information (PI), while in
HDDs/SSDs it typically points to a malformed CDB (often a software bug).
This is one of several possible error conditions.

Rather than introducing new BLK_STS_* codes, vendors have reused
existing ones for newer device types to maintain backward compatibility.

There isn't much we can do about this for now. I assume the malformed
CDB-related issues have largely been resolved, so this Btrfs fix is
effectively NVMe-specific.

Reviewed-by: Anand Jain <asj@kernel.org>

Thanks
Anand

> So handle the known expected errors and make some noise on the rest
> which we expect won't really happen.
> 
> Signed-off-by: Boris Burkov <boris@bur.io>
> ---
> Changelog:
> v3:
> - actually do the change stated in v2...
> v2:
> - proper bdev err logging for expected block errors
> - btrfs_warn_rl for all other errors
> 
> ---
>  fs/btrfs/bio.c | 12 ++++++++++--
>  1 file changed, 10 insertions(+), 2 deletions(-)
> 
> diff --git a/fs/btrfs/bio.c b/fs/btrfs/bio.c
> index 2a2a21aec817..9c3cd6978880 100644
> --- a/fs/btrfs/bio.c
> +++ b/fs/btrfs/bio.c
> @@ -4,6 +4,7 @@
>   * Copyright (C) 2022 Christoph Hellwig.
>   */
>  
> +#include "linux/blk_types.h"
>  #include <linux/bio.h>
>  #include "bio.h"
>  #include "ctree.h"
> @@ -350,11 +351,18 @@ static void btrfs_check_read_bio(struct btrfs_bio *bbio, struct btrfs_device *de
>  
>  static void btrfs_log_dev_io_error(const struct bio *bio, struct btrfs_device *dev)
>  {
> +	blk_status_t sts = bio->bi_status;
> +
>  	if (!dev || !dev->bdev)
>  		return;
> -	if (bio->bi_status != BLK_STS_IOERR && bio->bi_status != BLK_STS_TARGET)
> +	if (unlikely(sts == BLK_STS_OK))
>  		return;
> -
> +	if (unlikely(sts != BLK_STS_IOERR && sts != BLK_STS_TARGET &&
> +		     sts != BLK_STS_MEDIUM && sts != BLK_STS_PROTECTION)) {
> +		btrfs_warn_rl(dev->fs_info, "bdev %s unexpected block io error: %d",
> +			      btrfs_dev_name(dev), sts);
> +		return;
> +	}
>  	if (btrfs_op(bio) == BTRFS_MAP_WRITE)
>  		btrfs_dev_stat_inc_and_print(dev, BTRFS_DEV_STAT_WRITE_ERRS);
>  	else if (!(bio->bi_opf & REQ_RAHEAD))

review_comments.summary: David agrees with the patch and will add it to the for-next branch.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH] btrfs: add BTRFS_IOC_GET_CSUMS ioctl
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: The patch adds a new ioctl to retrieve checksums for Btrfs, but the discussion revolves around the approach and potential security implications.

review_comments.author: Boris Burkov
review_comments.summary: Raised security concerns about the approach, suggesting it might be insecure against certain attacks. However, acknowledged that the patch is not arbitrary and has some logical basis.

review_comments.author: Qu Wenruo
review_comments.summary: Clarified the approach and addressed some of Boris' concerns, but still expressed reservations about combining two tree search operations for privilege checks.
review_comments.raw_body:
在 2026/4/8 07:43, Boris Burkov 写道:
[...]
>>> We absolutely can't give non-root users csums to arbitrary data, that's
>>> definitely a security breach.
>>
>> If getting csums for random logical is a security breach, I do not think the
>> new GET_CSUM ioctl is any better.
>>
> 
> Isn't it better because you have to use a file we do permissions checks on?
> 
> So it's not an arbitrary logical, it's a logical used by a file you have
> access to? That might still be insecure against some attack, though, what
> do I know.

OK, that makes sense now. Although I'm still not a huge fan just to 
combine two different tree search operations into one, just to fulfill 
the privilege check requirement.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH] btrfs: abort transaction in do_remap_reloc_trans() on failure

patches_acked.subject: Re: [PATCH] btrfs: fix bytes_may_use leak in move_existing_remap()
Re: [PATCH] btrfs: fix bytes_may_use leak in do_remap_reloc_trans()

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v4 5/5] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint to spinning locks

version_history.subject: [PATCH v4 5/5] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint to spinning locks

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a contended_release tracepoint to queued spinlocks and rwlocks, allowing for tracing of lock contention events. The patch uses an arch-overridable function to ensure correct behavior across all architectures, including those with custom unlock implementations. When the tracepoint is disabled, it has minimal impact on performance, adding only a single NOP instruction to the hot path. However, when enabled, it can increase binary size by up to 0.24% due to the need to preserve the lock pointer in a callee-saved register across the trace call.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v4 4/5] locking: Factor out queued_spin_release()
patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new function called queued_spin_release(), which is an arch-overridable unlock primitive. It renames existing arch-specific queued_spin_unlock() overrides on x86 (paravirt) and MIPS to queued_spin_release(). The change is preparatory for the next commit, which will add contended release tracepoint instrumentation to queued_spin_unlock(). No functional changes are made.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v4 3/5] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint to sleepable locks

version_history.subject: [PATCH v4 3/5] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint to sleepable locks

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a new tracepoint called contended_release to the Linux kernel's locking subsystem. The contended_release tracepoint fires on the holder side when a contended lock is released, allowing for correlation of lock hold time under contention with waiter events by lock address. The patch modifies the slowpath unlock paths of various sleepable locks (mutex, rtmutex, semaphore, rwsem, percpu-rwsem, and RT-specific rwbase locks) to fire the tracepoint before releasing the lock or waking the waiter. This enables more detailed tracing and debugging of locking behavior in the kernel.

review_comments.author: Usama Arif
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Usama Arif pointed out a compilation error due to the use of 'waiter' variable outside its scope in the slowpath unlock path of sleepable locks, specifically in mutex.c.
review_comments.raw_body:
This won't compile? waiter is declared in the if block, so you are using
it outside scope here.

review_comments.author: Dmitry Ilvokhin (author)
review_comments.summary: Author addressed a concern about the scope of the waiter variable in the slowpath unlock paths, explaining that it is declared and assigned before the if block where the tracepoint is called.
review_comments.raw_body:
Thanks for the feedback, Usama.

waiter is declared at function scope, right on top. It's also assigned
before the if block, so it's still in scope at the tracepoint.

review_comments.author: Usama Arif
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that changes made on top of mm-new branch may have altered waiter declaration, potentially affecting contended_release tracepoint functionality.
review_comments.raw_body:
Ah ok, I was reviewing on top of mm-new branch from today where waiter
is declared in the if block. Probably something changed related to
locking/tracing and its not in mm-new yet.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v4 2/5] locking/percpu-rwsem: Extract __percpu_up_read()
patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch extracts the slowpath of percpu_up_read() into a new function __percpu_up_read(). The original percpu_up_read() is simplified and no longer contains the slowpath, which is moved to the new function. This change avoids increasing binary size due to the addition of a tracepoint to an inlined function. The patch also adds an export for the new function.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v4 0/5] locking: contended_release tracepoint instrumentation
patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series adds a new tracepoint called contended_release that fires on the lock holder's side when a lock is released while there are waiters. This provides two main benefits: it allows for hold time estimation by correlating contention_end and contended_release events, and it captures the holder's stack at release time, which can be different from what is captured earlier if the holder does significant work between the waiter's arrival and unlock. The patch also fixes several issues in the previous versions, including spurious events, use-after-free errors, and build failures on certain architectures.

review_comments.author: Matthew Wilcox
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Matthew Wilcox questioned the usefulness of the contended_release tracepoint, specifically asking for explanations on how it provides value in measuring hold time and capturing the holder's stack at release time.
review_comments.raw_body:
As someone who's not an expert in this area (so please use short words
to explain it to me), why do we want to know how long this holder took
to release the lock from when it became contended?

I understand why we want to know how long any given waiter had to wait
to gain the lock (but we already have tracepoints which show that).

I also don't understand why we want to know the holder's stack at
release time.  The stack at contention-begin time will include
the point at which the lock was acquired which should be correlated
with where the lock was released.

Perhaps examples might help me understand why we want this?

review_comments.author: Steven Rostedt
review_comments.summary:
Steven Rostedt noted that the tracepoint placement for queued spinlocks and rwlocks should fire before the lock is released, not after, to accurately capture the holder's stack at release time.

The reviewer, Steven Rostedt, pointed out that the contended_release tracepoint would be useful for determining how long a critical section is held when a lock is highly contended by many tasks. He suggested that seeing the release time and location would help identify if the length of the critical section is causing the contention.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Thu, 26 Mar 2026 15:55:21 +0000
Matthew Wilcox <willy@infradead.org> wrote:

---

Dmitry could give his own rationale for this, but I have my only use case.

This would be useful to find out how long the critical section is. If a
lock is highly contended by many tasks, you could get a high contention
time simply because other tasks are causing the delay for the waiter.
Seeing the release time and location would let you also know how long the
critical section was held, and if the length of the critical section is
causing the contention.

Having a stack trace of the release would differentiate the path that
released the lock, as there can be many places that release them. Although,
I have to admit, I'm not sure there are many different places locks are
released. Especially now that we have guard(), which will make all the
releases in a function at the same location.

-- Steve

review_comments.author: Dmitry Ilvokhin (author)
review_comments.summary:
The author is addressing Matthew Wilcox's suggestion to come up with generic instrumentation for contended release tracepoint, explaining that the current implementation provides a cause (holder time) and symptom (waiter time), but requires different treatments depending on the lock usage pattern.

The author addressed Matthew Wilcox's concern that the --lock-owner feature does not work for all lock types by explaining that contended_release tracepoint fires in the holder's context, providing the holder's stack at release time, which is necessary to identify the holders and what they were doing.
review_comments.raw_body:
I think the simplest way to think about it is the following. Waiter time
is the symptom, while holder time is the cause.

The waiter-side contention_begin/contention_end tells us how long a
waiter waited, but that time can span multiple holders.

If a waiter waited 10 ms, we can not tell whether one holder held the
lock for 10 ms or five holders held it for 2 ms each. These need
different treatments: the first means shrink the critical section, the
second means reduce lock frequency or split the lock. Today we can not
distinguish between these cases from waiter-side data alone.

---

Holder's stack allows us to understand who exactly waiters were waiting
for to release the lock.

The stack at contention_begin time does not always include the holder's
stack. The --lock-owner feature works by reading the owner field from
the lock struct, but it only supports mutex and rwsem. For spinlocks,
queued rwlocks, semaphores, and several others, the waiter has no
visibility into the holder whatsoever.

contended_release fires in the holder's context, so we get the holder's
stack at release time. For spinlocks, this is the only way to get any
holder-side information.

Original motivation was zone lock contention (a spinlock) in Meta
production workloads. We could see waiters were blocked, but had no way
to identify the holders or what they were doing.

review_comments.author: Usama Arif
review_comments.summary: Gave Acked-by

review_comments.author: Usama Arif
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Usama Arif suggested reordering the patches for better git bisectability and clarity in git blame, specifically moving refactoring work from patch 4 and 5 to earlier commits and adding all tracepoints in a single commit at the end.
review_comments.raw_body:
Would it be better to reorder the patches? It would help with git
bisectability as well. Move the refractoring work in patch 4 and
5 (excluding adding the tracepoints ofcourse) earlier, and then add
all the tracepoints in the same commit at the end? It would help
in the future with git blame to see where all the tracepoints
were added as well.

review_comments.author: Dmitry Ilvokhin (author)
review_comments.summary: Author addressed a concern about the scope of the 'waiter' variable by explaining that it is declared and assigned before the if block where the tracepoint is, thus still in scope.
review_comments.raw_body:
Thanks for the feedback, Usama.

waiter is declared at function scope, right on top. It's also assigned
before the if block, so it's still in scope at the tracepoint.

review_comments.author: Dmitry Ilvokhin (author)
review_comments.summary: The author is addressing a suggestion from Usama Arif to reorder the patch series so that each refactoring commit is followed by the commit that uses it, making the motivation for the refactoring clear and easing review. The author prefers to keep the current ordering, citing that bisectability should be fine as-is since each commit compiles and works independently.
review_comments.raw_body:
Thanks for the suggestion, Usama.

I'd prefer to keep the current ordering: each refactoring commit is
immediately followed by the commit that uses it. For example,
queued_spin_release() is factored out right before the commit that adds
the tracepoint to spinning locks. This makes the motivation for the
refactoring clear and should also ease the review since the context is
still fresh.

Bisectability should be fine as-is, each commit compiles and works
independently, since the refactoring patches do not introduce behavioral
changes on their own.

patches_reviewed.patch_summary: Discussion around adding unit-level tests for memory reclaim functionality, with concerns about performance regressions and the feasibility of using LLMs to generate test cases.

review_comments.author: Johannes Weiner
review_comments.summary: Suggested using a specific tool for generating test cases, implying that it could be a viable solution to the problem.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Tue, Apr 07, 2026 at 06:30:55PM +0100, Lorenzo Stoakes wrote:
> On Tue, Apr 07, 2026 at 12:56:31PM -0400, Gregory Price wrote:
> > Might as well consider the functional testing question as well as we
> > decide what those interfaces look like anyway.
> 
> Yes, adding unit-level tests would be hugely useful.

Do I know just the tool for generating these...

review_comments.author: Gregory Price
review_comments.summary: Expressed concerns about performance regressions and the feasibility of using LLMs, but also acknowledged the potential usefulness of unit-level tests.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Tue, Apr 07, 2026 at 06:30:55PM +0100, Lorenzo Stoakes wrote:
> 
> > somewhat trivial work these days via <pick your favorite LLM> with
> > reasonably well designed interfaces.
> 
> Though to be honest, I did ask Claude to do this with some code before and was
> really impressed with how quickly it came up with something.
> 
> Buuut I've generally found the code LLMs produce... terrible? Really terrible?
> So I think you'd want there to be some level of human improvement there.
> 
> And in any case, maybe doing things this way will struggle to really represent
> real world cases (lock contention etc.) but OTOH, being able to simulate a high
> memory pressure environment with certain parameters even without [the rest of
> the kernel] interacting might still be super super useful for diagnosing issues.x
>

There is a concern people start looking at these tests as signal for
performance regressions - which is neither meaningful nor useful
(unless you fundamentally break list iteration or something).

But for trivial cases, you'd at least like to know under strongly
controlled aging and reclaim scenarios that anon/file memory is
reclaimed in a balanced manner - just as an example. 

If something breaks THAT assumption, you know there's a logic issue.

Likewise with comparisons between LRU and MGLRU.  It might be easier to
identify the behavioral divergences that way.

But i fear the whole page-table walk thing makes all of this a
non-starter.  That's turns into stubbing out like rmap and such.

~Gregory

review_comments.author: Lorenzo Stoakes
review_comments.summary: Initially suggested using LLMs to generate test cases, but later expressed concerns about the quality of the generated code and the potential for performance regressions.

Buuut I've generally found the code LLMs produce... terrible? Really terrible?
So I think you'd want there to be some level of human improvement there.

And in any case, maybe doing things this way will struggle to really represent
real world cases (lock contention etc.) but OTOH, being able to simulate a high
memory pressure environment with certain parameters even without [the rest of
the kernel] interacting might still be super super useful for diagnosing issues.x

>
>    ( read: probably not with the current api n_n;; ).

*gasp*! But yeah, also yes.

>
> So to get there, we'd need to agree on the refactor work and interfaces
> that make it possible to model the scenarios outside a running kernel.
>
> If we do actually want to move the core to look like:
>   - vmscan.c  : core library
>   - lru.c     : standard lru
>   - mglru.c   : mglru
>
> Might as well consider the functional testing question as well as we
> decide what those interfaces look like anyway.

Yes, adding unit-level tests would be hugely useful.

>
> *throws the pipe into the bikeshed*

*Dives in bikeshed to rescue*

>
> ~Gregory

Cheers, Lorenzo

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH] md/raid0: use kvzalloc/kvfree for strip_zone and devlist allocations
Re: [PATCH v2 16/20] vfio/cxl: Register regions with VFIO layer

patches_acked.subject: Re: [PATCH] mm/mempolicy: Fix weighted interleave auto sysfs name
patches_acked.patch_summary: Patch to fix weighted interleave auto sysfs name, addressing a cosmetic issue introduced by a previous commit.

review_comments.author: Gregory Price
review_comments.summary: Identified potential breakage of userland software due to the patch's changes, suggested adding a Fixes tag for the original commit that introduced 'auto'.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Tue, Apr 07, 2026 at 10:23:50AM -0700, Joshua Hahn wrote:
> On Tue, 7 Apr 2026 13:09:04 -0400 Gregory Price <gourry@gourry.net> wrote:
> 
> > > Signed-off-by: Joshua Hahn <joshua.hahnjy@gmail.com>
> > 
> > Maybe - 
> > 
> > Fixes: 2fb6915fa22d ("compiler_types.h: add "auto" as a macro for "__auto_type"")
> 
> I thought about adding a Fixes tag, decided against it because it's
> purely cosmetic.
> 
> ... but now that I think about it, I think this could break some workflows
> for users who have automated writing to the auto sysfs file.
>

Yes, it broke userland software depending on that symbol being there
(which, hopefully, is very little at this point in time).

> Maybe I should add a fixes tag for the patch that introduced the 
> sysfs file instead? That is,
> 
> Fixes: e341f9c3c841 ("mm/mempolicy: Weighted Interleave Auto-tuning")
>

The original commit introduced 'auto' and the compiler_types.h commit
modified that to "__auto_type".  The latter broke userland so that's the
right commit to mark for Fixes.

~Gregory

review_comments.author: Joshua Hahn
review_comments.summary: Acknowledged Gregory's concerns and suggested adding a Fixes tag for the original commit that modified 'auto' to '__auto_type'.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Tue, 7 Apr 2026 13:09:04 -0400 Gregory Price <gourry@gourry.net> wrote:

> On Tue, Apr 07, 2026 at 07:14:14AM -0700, Joshua Hahn wrote:
> > The __ATTR macro is a utility that makes defining kobj_attributes easier
> > by stringfying the name, verifying the mode, and setting the show/store
> > fields in a single initializer. It takes a raw token as the first value,
> > rather than a string, so that __ATTR family macros like __ATTR_RW can
> > token-paste it for inferring the _show / _store function names.
> > 
> > Commit e341f9c3c841 ("mm/mempolicy: Weighted Interleave Auto-tuning")
> > used the __ATTR macro to define the "auto" sysfs for weighted
> > interleave. A few months later, commit 2fb6915fa22d ("compiler_types.h:
> > add "auto" as a macro for "__auto_type"") introduced a #define macro
> > which expanded auto into __auto_type.
> > 
> 

Hello Gregory, thanks for reviewing my patch!!

> Well that's really unfortunately.
> 
> Is this really the only "auto" in the entire sysfs structure?

Thankfully, it seems so:

$ rg "__ATTR\(auto"

mm/mempolicy.c
3786:   __ATTR(auto, 0664, weighted_interleave_auto_show,

arch/powerpc/platforms/pseries/power.c
44:     __ATTR(auto_poweron, 0644, auto_poweron_show, auto_poweron_store);

Although maybe it is more valuable to check any __ATTR family macro
for a macro as the first parameter.  In hindsight, I should have been more
wary of passing a keyword as a token.

[...snip...]

> > Signed-off-by: Joshua Hahn <joshua.hahnjy@gmail.com>
> 
> Maybe - 
> 
> Fixes: 2fb6915fa22d ("compiler_types.h: add "auto" as a macro for "__auto_type"")

I thought about adding a Fixes tag, decided against it because it's
purely cosmetic.

... but now that I think about it, I think this could break some workflows
for users who have automated writing to the auto sysfs file.

Maybe I should add a fixes tag for the patch that introduced the 
sysfs file instead? That is,

Fixes: e341f9c3c841 ("mm/mempolicy: Weighted Interleave Auto-tuning")

> Reviewed-by: Gregory Price <gourry@gourry.net>

Thanks again, Gregory!! I hope you have a great day : -)
Joshua

review_comments.author: Gregory Price
review_comments.summary: Confirmed that the original commit introduced 'auto' and the subsequent commit modified it to '__auto_type', which broke userland software.
review_comments.raw_body:

> >

> 
> I thought about adding a Fixes tag, decided against it because it's

Yes, it broke userland software depending on that symbol being there
